title: Endurance and Rediscovery: The Royal Ballet of Cambodia
highlights: Anciently, this type of performance was reserved for the royal courts of Cambodia, where it accompanied coronations, marriages, and funerals. Eventually the dancers of the Royal Ballet of Cambodia began to perform internationally, starting with the 1906 Colonial Exposition in France. The Ballet performed in Europe periodically until political unrest devastated the country in 1975. In April of that year, a violent, radical group called the Khmer Rouge took over Cambodia. In an effort to transform the country into a perfectly equal communist state, the Khmer Rouge abolished  ...    had mostly been destroyed during the genocide, the artists’ deaths were devastating to Cambodian culture. The few dancers who survived were eager to reconstruct the traditional repertory. Notable among them was Her Royal Highness the Princess Norodom Buppha Devi, a gifted lifelong student of Khmer classical dance. title: Em Theay
highlights: 75, she talks about her happy childhood in Phnom Penh and how her gift for dancing later helped her survive the brutality of the Khmer  ...    One afternoon, as I was walking out of the palace, I heard the Khmer Rouge had taken over Phnom Penh. I was carrying my  ...    taking over. With my family, I started the march to Phnom Penh. We had no shoes, so it took us over a month. Of my 18 children, 13 died in those four years. And of the 300 or 400 dancers at the royal palace before the Khmer Rouge, only six of us survived. Some starved; some were executed. The Khmer Rouge deliberately killed artists and educated people, so our culture was badly weakened.
